headline: Berlin, Beijing, Tehran: the year Europe's Cold War ended and other worlds turned
summary: 1989 was one of the great inflection points of the twentieth century. In the space of a few months a wave of largely peaceful revolutions ended communist rule in Poland, Hungary, East Germany, Czechoslovakia, Bulgaria and Romania; the Berlin Wall was breached on 9 November after an East German press-conference blunder. In China the Beijing spring ended in the massacre around Tiananmen Square on the night of 3-4 June. In Iran Ayatollah Ruhollah Khomeini died on 3 June; his successor Ali Khamenei took over. The Soviet army completed its withdrawal from Afghanistan; Namibia moved toward independence; F. W. de Klerk began the negotiations that would end apartheid; and Tim Berners-Lee at CERN drafted the proposal for what would become the World Wide Web.

snapshots.text: Europe in 1989 lived through the fall of its Iron Curtain. In February the Polish government under General Wojciech Jaruzelski opened Round Table talks with Solidarity's Lech Walesa; partly free elections on 4 June returned a Solidarity landslide, and Tadeusz Mazowiecki became Eastern Europe's first non-communist prime minister on 24 August. On 2 May Hungary began dismantling its border fence with Austria; on 19 August a Pan-European Picnic at Sopron let hundreds of East Germans cross. The exodus destabilised the GDR under Erich Honecker, who was ousted by Egon Krenz on 18 October. On 9 November, at a chaotic press conference, Politburo spokesman Guenter Schabowski announced that new travel regulations took effect immediately; East Berliners poured through Checkpoint Charlie and clambered atop the Berlin Wall. The Velvet Revolution began in Prague on 17 November after riot police beat student demonstrators; Vaclav Havel became president on 29 December. Bulgaria's Todor Zhivkov was toppled on 10 November. In Romania the Christmas revolution overthrew and executed Nicolae Ceausescu on 25 December. Mikhail Gorbachev, still promoting perestroika and glasnost, met George Bush at Malta on 2-3 December in a summit that declared the Cold War over.

In the United States George H. W. Bush was inaugurated on 20 January; his administration would negotiate the end of the Cold War, launch Operation Just Cause to invade Panama and arrest Manuel Noriega on 20 December, and preside over the beginning of the savings and loan crisis. On 4 March 34 people were killed in the crash of United Airlines Flight 811 over the Pacific; on 24 March the Exxon Valdez ran aground on Bligh Reef in Prince William Sound and spilled 260,000 barrels of crude, one of the worst oil spills in American history. In Ottawa Prime Minister Brian Mulroney's Conservative government implemented the Canada-US Free Trade Agreement. In Mexico Carlos Salinas de Gortari, elected in the disputed 1988 poll, opened his sexenio of neoliberal reform. Brazil elected Fernando Collor de Mello in the country's first direct presidential vote since 1960, on 17 December. In Chile the Pinochet dictatorship, having lost the 1988 plebiscite, lost the December election to Patricio Aylwin. In El Salvador the FMLN offensive of November demonstrated the war's stalemate; six Jesuit priests, their housekeeper and her daughter were murdered at the Central American University on 16 November by an army unit.

China in 1989 saw the largest popular protest of the reform era and its violent suppression. The death of the reformist former general secretary Hu Yaobang on 15 April triggered student mourning at Tiananmen Square that grew into a movement of hundreds of thousands demanding political reform, press freedom and an end to corruption. On 13 May students began a hunger strike as Mikhail Gorbachev arrived in Beijing for the first Sino-Soviet summit in three decades. On 20 May the government declared martial law. On the night of 3-4 June units of the 27th and 38th Armies moved into central Beijing; the death toll around the square and along Chang'an Avenue is disputed but was probably in the hundreds to low thousands. The general secretary Zhao Ziyang, who had opposed the crackdown, was purged and replaced by Jiang Zemin. In Japan Emperor Hirohito died on 7 January after a 62-year reign; his son Akihito succeeded him and inaugurated the Heisei era. Recruit and other scandals crippled the LDP government of Noboru Takeshita, who resigned in June. The Nikkei stock index reached an all-time peak of 38,915.87 on 29 December, on the eve of the collapse of Japan's bubble economy.

In South and Central Asia 1989 was defined by the withdrawal of the Soviet army from Afghanistan. Under the terms of the 1988 Geneva accords, and after almost a decade of war that had killed perhaps a million Afghans and displaced five million, the last Soviet soldier, Lieutenant General Boris Gromov, crossed the Termez bridge over the Amu Darya on 15 February. The regime of Mohammed Najibullah held on longer than expected while mujahideen factions fought each other. In India the Congress government of Rajiv Gandhi went to the polls in November amid the Bofors bribery scandal; it lost, and V. P. Singh's National Front took office on 2 December with support from both the BJP and the left. In Kashmir the outbreak of an armed insurgency began after the abduction on 8 December of the daughter of home minister Mufti Mohammad Sayeed by the JKLF. In Sri Lanka the JVP insurrection was crushed with massive extrajudicial killings; the Indian Peacekeeping Force withdrew from the Tamil war. In Pakistan Benazir Bhutto, sworn in in December 1988, governed under President Ghulam Ishaq Khan. In Iran, on 3 June, Ayatollah Ruhollah Khomeini, the founder of the Islamic Republic, died at eighty-six; the Assembly of Experts elected Ali Khamenei as his successor the next day, and Akbar Hashemi Rafsanjani became president in July.

The Middle East and North Africa in 1989 were reshaping around the exhaustion of the eight-year Iran-Iraq war, ended by ceasefire the previous August. On 14 February Ayatollah Khomeini issued a fatwa calling for the death of the British-Indian novelist Salman Rushdie for his novel The Satanic Verses; the affair rippled through global politics for years. Khomeini himself died on 3 June. On 22 October the Taif Agreement, negotiated among Lebanese parliamentarians in Saudi Arabia, laid the framework for ending Lebanon's fifteen-year civil war and rebalanced political representation between Christian and Muslim confessions. In the Palestinian territories the First Intifada, which had erupted in December 1987, continued. In Egypt Hosni Mubarak's regime pursued limited liberalisation. In Libya Muammar Gaddafi negotiated the Great Man-Made River. In Algeria President Chadli Bendjedid's constitutional reform of February legalised opposition parties for the first time since independence, opening the door to the electoral rise of the Islamic Salvation Front and, ultimately, the civil war of the 1990s. In Iraq Saddam Hussein turned toward Kuwait, laying grievances that would become the invasion of 2 August 1990. In Israel the Likud-Labour national unity government under Yitzhak Shamir presided over a wave of Soviet Jewish immigration.

Southern Africa in 1989 moved with startling speed toward the end of settler rule. In South Africa the National Party under P. W. Botha replaced its leader with F. W. de Klerk on 15 August; on his inauguration on 20 September de Klerk signalled a break with the securocrat strategy of the 1980s. On 22 October eight ANC-aligned prisoners including Walter Sisulu were released from Robben Island and Pollsmoor; Nelson Mandela himself would be freed on 11 February 1990 and the ANC unbanned on 2 February. In Namibia (South West Africa), under UN Security Council Resolution 435 of 1978 finally implemented after the 1988 tripartite accords, elections to a Constituent Assembly on 7-11 November returned a two-thirds SWAPO majority; Namibia would achieve independence on 21 March 1990 under President Sam Nujoma. Angola's civil war between the MPLA government and Jonas Savimbi's UNITA continued. In Ethiopia Mengistu Haile Mariam's Derg regime tottered before EPLF and TPLF advances. In Somalia the Barre regime was fragmenting; famine and civil war lay ahead. In Nigeria General Ibrahim Babangida promised a transition to civilian rule that would culminate in the annulled election of June 1993.

Australia in 1989 was governed by Bob Hawke's fourth Labor government, re-elected in March 1990. The domestic year was dominated by the pilots' dispute of August-October, when Australian Airline Pilots Federation members resigned en masse over wage claims and Hawke's government sided with the airlines, effectively breaking the union. Treasurer Paul Keating's tight monetary policy pushed rates above 17 percent. The states of NSW under Nick Greiner and Victoria under John Cain grappled with the fallout of the 1987 crash and the Pyramid Building Society collapse. Aboriginal Reconciliation Council legislation was drafted for 1991. New Zealand's fourth Labour government under David Lange, Geoffrey Palmer and, from 4 September, Mike Moore, continued to implement Rogernomics; the sale of state assets under Roger Douglas had split Labour and would sink it in the 1990 election. In Fiji, in the wake of Sitiveni Rabuka's 1987 coups, the country was governed under an interim regime writing an ethnic Fijian-favoured constitution. Vanuatu under Father Walter Lini and Papua New Guinea under Rabbie Namaliu grappled with the Bougainville rebellion, which had erupted in November 1988 around the Panguna copper mine and would force its closure in 1989.

sources.title: The Revolutions of 1989
